primary_diagnosis.name: Severe statin-associated muscle symptoms with high risk of statin-induced myopathy/rhabdomyolysis, precipitated by a clarithromycin-atorvastatin (CYP3A4) drug interaction

pathophysiology_summary: Statins can cause dose-dependent skeletal-muscle toxicity ranging from mild myalgia to severe myopathy and, at the extreme, rhabdomyolysis with myoglobinuria and acute kidney injury. Two compounding factors put this patient at high risk: (1) the atorvastatin dose was recently doubled to a high dose (80 mg daily), and (2) concurrent clarithromycin — a potent inhibitor of CYP3A4, the enzyme that metabolises atorvastatin — sharply increases atorvastatin plasma exposure. The result is symmetrical proximal myopathy (thighs, buttocks, shoulders) with functional collapse. Although urine is currently clear (no gross myoglobinuria) the creatine kinase may already be rising; untreated, muscle breakdown can release myoglobin and precipitate acute kidney injury. The definitive management is immediate cessation of the statin AND the interacting macrolide, urgent CK and renal-function testing, hydration and monitoring; symptoms typically resolve over weeks once the offending drugs are stopped.

differential.evidence_basis: Symmetrical proximal muscle aching and weakness in an older adult on a cholesterol-lowering drug is the classic pattern of statin myopathy — but the medication history must be elicited to establish it.

Also causes symmetrical proximal (shoulder/hip-girdle) aching and stiffness in older adults; kept in view until the temporal link to a statin dose change and an interacting antibiotic makes a drug cause far more likely.
The feared endpoint of severe statin myotoxicity; must be actively screened for (urine colour, CK, renal function) even though urine is currently clear.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, a drug cause should already be near the top given the presenting picture, but the diagnosis is not secure until the medication timeline is taken.

differential.diagnosis: statin-associated muscle symptoms / statin-induced myopathy (statin-macrolide interaction)
differential.evidence_basis: Onset a few weeks after the statin dose was doubled, plus concurrent macrolide antibiotic (a potent CYP3A4 inhibitor), makes a pharmacological cause overwhelmingly likely.
Becomes less likely once the clear chronological link to a statin dose increase and an interacting antibiotic is established.
Can cause symmetrical proximal weakness; usually more subacute and without the tight temporal drug link — retained as an alternative pending CK and specialist assessment.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Eliciting BOTH the statin dose increase (DI-002) and the concurrent macrolide (DI-003) is the pivotal reasoning step; missing the antibiotic misses the interaction driving toxicity.

differential.diagnosis: statin-induced myopathy at high risk of rhabdomyolysis (statin-macrolide interaction)
differential.evidence_basis: Severe proximal myopathy with functional collapse and a potent drug interaction; rhabdomyolysis/AKI risk remains until CK and renal function are known.

differential.diagnosis: acute spinal cord compression / cauda equina
differential.evidence_basis: No sensory level, no numbness/pins-and-needles, no bladder or bowel dysfunction (DI-006) — argues strongly against a compressive cord lesion.
Hypothyroidism can cause proximal myopathy and raised CK; a reasonable check (TSH) but does not explain the acute onset tied to the medication change.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Pertinent negatives (clear urine, no neuro deficit) refine risk but do NOT downgrade urgency: CK and renal bloods are still required to exclude rhabdomyolysis.

differential.diagnosis: severe statin-associated muscle symptoms / statin-induced myopathy from a clarithromycin-atorvastatin interaction, high risk of rhabdomyolysis
differential.evidence_basis: Classic timeline, potent CYP3A4 interaction, severe proximal myopathy; definitive action is cessation of both offending drugs plus urgent CK/renal testing.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Telehealth cannot confirm CK/renal status — the correct disposition is urgent same-day in-person assessment with bloods, not a remote reassurance.

red_flags.description: Recently doubled high-dose statin taken together with a macrolide antibiotic (a potent CYP3A4 inhibitor) — a high-risk drug-drug interaction driving statin toxicity
red_flags.clinical_significance: The interaction markedly raises statin exposure and the risk of severe myopathy and rhabdomyolysis; both drugs must be stopped.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Continued co-administration allows escalating muscle breakdown, myoglobinuria and acute kidney injury.

red_flags.description: Severe symmetrical proximal muscle weakness with loss of independent mobility
red_flags.clinical_significance: Marks severe (not trivial) myopathy and signals significant muscle injury requiring urgent evaluation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Dismissing it as benign myalgia delays cessation of the offending drugs and blood testing.

red_flags.description: Risk of rhabdomyolysis with myoglobinuria and acute kidney injury (urine currently clear, but CK/renal status unknown)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Rhabdomyolysis is the life- and kidney-threatening endpoint; a normal urine colour does not exclude a rising CK or evolving AKI.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Undetected rhabdomyolysis can cause acute kidney injury and electrolyte disturbance.

red_flags.description: Older adult with abrupt loss of mobility and high falls risk at home (unable to transfer without assistance; unsafe low seating)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Sudden functional collapse in a frail older adult adds an immediate injury/fall hazard alongside the drug-toxicity risk.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A fall or inability to self-care overnight could cause serious injury independent of the underlying myopathy.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Dismissing symmetrical proximal aching in an older adult as strain, viral myalgia or ageing, and under-triaging — missing severe statin myopathy.

Recognising a possible statin cause but failing to elicit and act on the concurrent macrolide antibiotic that is amplifying statin toxicity; stopping the statin alone while the interacting antibiotic continues is incomplete.
Treating a normal urine colour as proof against rhabdomyolysis; CK and renal function are still required because early muscle injury can precede visible myoglobinuria.
Attempting to fully exclude rhabdomyolysis or reassure remotely; the CK/renal bloods and hands-on assessment that settle risk cannot be done over chat, so urgent in-person review is required.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Objective muscle power on formal examination
Accurate blood pressure and heart rate without a home device
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Advise immediate cessation of the statin and arrange urgent SAME-DAY in-person medical review for CK, renal function and LFTs; advise that the interacting macrolide antibiotic be stopped and, if antibiotic treatment is still needed, substituted with a non-interacting agent by a prescriber. Escalate to an emergency department if dark/tea-coloured urine, reduced urine output, worsening or spreading weakness, vomiting, confusion or collapse occur.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the clinical label given the classic timeline and interaction; the real danger is UNDER-action — dismissing it as benign, failing to stop the interacting antibiotic, or reassuring remotely instead of arranging urgent bloods, allowing progression to rhabdomyolysis and acute kidney injury.

guidelines_referenced.reference: General statin-safety / myopathy management principles (Australian practice)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Cease the statin and any interacting CYP3A4 inhibitor; check creatine kinase and renal function; consider and exclude rhabdomyolysis where CK is markedly elevated or myoglobinuria is present; reassess lipid management once recovered.
